headline: Montana Day at New York World's Fair, 1964
body:
Well, now, if you ever found yourself in the bustling heart of New York City, amidst the grand spectacle of the 1964 World's Fair, you might have stumbled upon a curious sight on April 24th. It was Montana Day, they called it, a celebration of the Big Sky Country, a hundred years since it first became a territory. And let me tell you, Montana, bless its vast, untamed heart, wasn't about to let this opportunity slip by unnoticed. They brought a piece of the frontier right to Flushing Meadows, a bold declaration of their place in the Union.

The exhibit itself was a marvel of rustic ingenuity, a stark contrast to the gleaming, futuristic domes and spires that dotted the fairgrounds. Seven railroad cars, mind you, parked on a siding, each one a canvas for the Montana artist Lyman A. Rice, who'd painted sixteen-foot murals of Western scenes upon their sides. Three of these cars, they said, housed a museum, a veritable treasure trove of Montana's past. Another displayed a collection of cattle brands, a testament to the enduring spirit of the open range. And scattered about, for good measure, were two corrals, two log forts, and, as the official description put it, "some Indians." It was a tableau designed to stir the imagination, to transport the city-dweller to a land of rugged beauty and boundless opportunity.

Inside those museum cars, one could find mounted examples of Montana's fish and wildlife, alongside the works of those famed Western painters, Charles M. Russell and Frederic Remington. And then there was the gold, a million dollars' worth of nuggets and dust, piled up casually, a heap no bigger than a cubic foot. It was enough to make a man's eyes widen and his thoughts drift to the days of the gold rush, when fortunes were made and lost with the turn of a shovel. There were also fourteen cases from the Don Foote collection, filled with mementos of Western personalities: guns, saddles, and bridles once belonging to the likes of Wild Bill Hickok, General George Armstrong Custer, and Buffalo Bill Cody. A true glimpse into the rough-and-tumble history of the West.

The corrals offered their own brand of entertainment. One held a stuffed pinto horse, frozen in a bucking position, inviting fairgoers to mount it for a photograph, a chance to pretend, if only for a moment, they were wrangling a wild mustang. The other corral showcased the various breeds of livestock that thrived in Montana's fertile lands. And, of course, there were the tribal dances performed by the encamped Indians, a vibrant display of culture and tradition. In the arcade of one of the forts, nickelodeons and other antique music-making machines played their tunes, all in working order, ready to transport visitors back to an earlier era.

But the true star of Montana's centennial push was the Centennial Train, a rolling ambassador that carried the spirit of the Big Sky Country across the nation. It was a grand idea, born from the desire to showcase Montana's heritage and attract tourism and investment. This train, a symbol of progress and connection, had made its way to New York for the occasion, further emphasizing Montana's presence on the national stage. It was a journey that brought the vastness of the West to the crowded East, a bridge between two worlds. As one woman, recalling her experience at the fair, was quoted as saying, “But no one ever came near me.” She added, with a mischievous glint, “I could have shot ‘em.” A sentiment, perhaps, that spoke to the lingering wildness that Montana, even in its centennial year, still held dear.

Montana Day at the World's Fair was more than just an exhibit; it was a statement, a proud declaration of a state's identity, a blend of its rugged past and its promising future. It was a moment when the grandeur of the West met the marvels of modernity, a fleeting glimpse of a land that continued to capture the imagination of a nation. The fair, with its millions of visitors, provided an unparalleled platform for Montana to tell its story, to invite the world to experience its unique charm, and to solidify its place in the American narrative. It was a spectacle, a celebration, and a shrewd piece of promotion, all rolled into one unforgettable day.
location: New York City, New York County
